fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any bijection f\colon A\to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.
Any invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a section.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a retraction r\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r\colon r\circ f\sim 1_{A} exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a retraction.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a section s\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s\colon f\circ s\sim 1_{B} exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a biinvertible.
Because contractible maps are both sections and retractions.
Any quasi-invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
Any quasi-inverse can be improved to a coherent inverse, and any coherently invertible map is contractible.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.

Let f\colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the postcomposition map \mathopen {}\left \lparen f\circ -\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen C\to A\right \rparen \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lparen C\to B\right \rparen \mathclose {} is biinvertible.

The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.

Let f\colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the precomposition map \mathopen {}\left \lparen -\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen B\to C\right \rparen \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lparen A\to C\right \rparen \mathclose {} is biinvertible.
Analogous to the corresponding result for pushforward along biinvertible maps.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A \to B is propositional.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is surjective.
This is immediate, using the unit map of propositional truncation and the section of f.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is a bijection.
By and .
Any embedding f\colon A\to B is propositional.

We must check that for every b:B, the fiber of f at b is a proposition. Fixing u_0,u_1:\mathsf {fiber}\,f\,b we must check that u_0=u_1. We let \mathopen {}\left \lparen a_0,p_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} \equiv u_0 and \mathopen {}\left \lparen a_1,p_1\right \rparen \mathclose {} \equiv u_1 so that we have p_0\colon fa_0=b and p_1\colon fa_1=b. Invoking the path object structure on fibers (), it suffices to exhibit the following data:
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0=\mathsf {act}_{f}\phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\phi = p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.
Any propositional map f\colon A\to B is an embedding.

Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By and , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{f} (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).

We first define the sections s_{x,y}\colon fx = fy \to x = y. Because f is assumed to be propositional, we have by an assignment \phi _{x,y}: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,h^{-1}\right \rparen \mathclose {} . We therefore define s_{x,y}h :\equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen \phi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} .1; the witness of sectionhood can be constructed using \mathopen {}\left \lparen \phi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} .2 modulo some identification algebra.

To construct the retractions r_{x,y}\colon fx=fy\to x = y, we note by our assumption that we have an assignment \psi _{x,y}:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} =_{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,h^{-1}\right \rparen \mathclose {} , and we define r_{x,y}h :\equiv \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} . It remains to show that for all x,y:A and h\colon x =y, we have \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} =h. By induction on h it suffices to show that for all x:A we have \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,x}\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} = \mathsf {refl}. We will deduce that \psi _{x,x}\mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} from our assumption that f\colon A\to B is propositional and thus fibred in sets via .
Any biinvertible map is an embedding.
By and .
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is biinvertible.
By , bijections are contractible; by , bijections are contractible.
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is biinvertible.
By , bijections are contractible; by , bijections are contractible.
Any embedding f\colon A\to B is propositional.

We must check that for every b:B, the fiber of f at b is a proposition. Fixing u_0,u_1:\mathsf {fiber}\,f\,b we must check that u_0=u_1. We let \mathopen {}\left \lparen a_0,p_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} \equiv u_0 and \mathopen {}\left \lparen a_1,p_1\right \rparen \mathclose {} \equiv u_1 so that we have p_0\colon fa_0=b and p_1\colon fa_1=b. Invoking the path object structure on fibers (), it suffices to exhibit the following data:
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0=\mathsf {act}_{f}\phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\phi = p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.
A function f\colon A\to B is called bijective when it is a surjective propositional map.

For any type A we have a proposition \mathsf {toProp}\,{A} equipped with a unit map \eta _{\mathsf {prop}}\colon A \to \mathsf {toProp}\,{A}; moreover, we have the following induction principle: for any family of propositions B\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A \to \mathsf {Prop}, any function b\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} B \mathopen {}\left \lparen \eta _{\mathsf {prop}}\,x\right \rparen \mathclose {} extends to a function b^\sharp \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} Bx.
When we have an element of \mathsf {toProp}\,A, we will say that A is inhabited.
A function f\colon A\to B is called surjective when for any b\colon B, the fiber of f over b is inhabited.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
